title: Building a Time Tracking & Timesheets App in Laravel
subtitle: Running timers, time entries, overlap handling, billable rates and rounding, approval workflows, and reporting — the Laravel architecture behind a time-tracking tool.

sections.h2: How a time tracker fits together

blocks.p: A time tracker is a log of time intervals attached to billable work, then rolled up and approved. The pressure: a timer can be running (no end yet), entries must not silently overlap, billing depends on rates and rounding rules, and timesheets flow through approval before invoicing.

blocks.diagram:
User
 ├─ Projects ─► Tasks (billable?, rate)
 ├─ Time Entries (start, end|null, duration, billable, rate)
 │     └─ running entry = end IS NULL
 ├─ Timesheet (a period's entries) ─► approval workflow
 └─ Reports (hours by project/user/day, billable totals)

blocks.p: The defining action — stopping a timer — closes the interval and computes billable value:

blocks.diagram:
Stop timer ──► PATCH /entries/{id}/stop
   │ entry.end = now()
   ▼ duration = end - start  (apply rounding rule)
   ▼ billable_amount = round(duration) * rate  (if billable)
   │
   └─► update running-timer state; recompute day/period totals (cached)

ul: Time entry model — intervals with a nullable end (running) tied to a task/project.
Integrity rules — one running timer per user, overlap detection, rounding.
Billing — billable flag, rate resolution, rounding policy → amount.
Approval & reporting — timesheet submission/approval and aggregate hours.

blocks.callout: A time tracker is an interval log with billing rules and an approval gate. The hard parts are running-timer state, overlap/rounding correctness, and fast aggregation — not basic CRUD.
blocks.title: The mental model in one sentence

blocks.h3: Time entries and the running timer

blocks.p: A time entry has started_at and a nullable ended_at. A running timer is simply an entry with ended_at = null. Duration is computed (ended_at - started_at), or now() - started_at while running. Don't store a separate 'is_running' flag — the null end is the single source of truth.

Most trackers enforce one active timer per user. Starting a new timer auto-stops the previous. Enforce with a guard (and ideally a partial unique index on user_id where ended_at is null) so concurrent start requests can't create two running entries.

Manually-entered entries can overlap (logging 9–11 and 10–12). Decide policy: reject overlaps, or allow and flag. Detect with an interval query: two intervals overlap when start < other.end AND end > other.start. This must run on create/edit, scoped to the user.

Billing often rounds durations — to the nearest 6 minutes (0.1h), 15 minutes, or up to a minimum. Rounding is a policy applied at calculation time, not stored raw. Keep the true duration and apply rounding when computing billable amounts so you can change the rule later.

The billable rate can come from the task, the project, the user, or a client contract — with a precedence order. Resolve it explicitly (task rate ?? project rate ?? user default) at entry time, and snapshot it onto the entry so later rate changes don't rewrite history.

Entries for a period form a timesheet that a user submits; a manager approves or rejects (back to draft). Lock approved entries against edits. This is a small state machine (draft → submitted → approved/rejected) gating downstream invoicing.

Reports aggregate hours by project, user, day, and billable status. Aggregate in SQL (sum, group by) over an indexed entries table; cache heavy rollups. For a 'running total today' use a cheap query against today's entries plus the live timer delta computed in the app.

blocks.callout: Storing duration as a mutable field that you increment is the classic bug — clock skew, missed stops, and double counting corrupt it. Derive duration from started_at/ended_at; keep the running timer as a null end.
blocks.title: Most common misconception

blocks.p: A time tracker logs intervals (running = null end), enforces one active timer and no silent overlaps, applies rounding and rate rules to compute billable amounts, and gates timesheets through approval before reporting/invoicing.

A time entry is a parking session: it starts when you arrive and the duration is just arrival-to-departure — you wouldn't keep a running 'minutes' counter that could drift, you'd read the two clock stamps. You can only occupy one space at a time (one running timer); double-parking (overlap) is flagged. The fee rounds to the next block and uses the posted rate at the time you parked (rate snapshot). At month end the lot manager reviews and approves the tickets before billing.

blocks.diagram:
Existing: 09:00 ───────── 11:00
New:           10:00 ───────── 12:00
Overlap when  newStart < existEnd  AND  newEnd > existStart
              10:00   < 11:00     AND  12:00  > 09:00  → OVERLAP

blocks.p: Each lesson is one idea. Code illustrative.

blocks.h3: Lesson 3 — Detect overlaps on manual entry
Lesson 4 — Apply rounding at calc time
Lesson 5 — Resolve and snapshot the rate

items.q: Q1. How is a running timer represented, and why not an is_running flag?
items.a: As a time entry with ended_at = null. The null end is the single source of truth; a separate flag can desync from reality (e.g. set true while end is also set). Duration derives from started_at to ended_at (or now).

items.q: Q2. How do you guarantee only one active timer per user under concurrent requests?
items.a: Auto-stop the previous running entry inside a transaction on start, backed by a partial unique index on user_id WHERE ended_at IS NULL so the database rejects a second concurrent running row.
Two intervals overlap when newStart < existingEnd AND newEnd > existingStart. Run this query scoped to the user on create/edit of manual entries.

items.q: Q4. Why apply rounding at calculation time rather than storing rounded duration?
items.a: Rounding is a billing policy that may change. Keep the true duration; apply the rounding rule when computing billable amounts so history isn't corrupted and the rule can be revised.

items.q: Q5. Why snapshot the rate onto the entry?
items.a: So later changes to task/project/user rates don't retroactively rewrite past billable amounts. Resolve precedence (task ?? project ?? user) once and store it on the entry.

items.q: Q6. What does the timesheet approval workflow protect?
items.a: It gates entries before invoicing: draft → submitted → approved/rejected. Approved entries are locked against edits, ensuring billed time isn't silently changed.

items.q: Q7. How should 'hours this week by project' be computed?
items.a: In SQL with SUM of (ended_at - started_at), COALESCE-ing now() for running entries, grouped by project, over an indexed entries table — not by looping entries in PHP.

blocks.p: Re-do the quiz from memory; redraw the overlap test each session.

head: When
Session (~10–15 min)
Goal
rows: Read packet; quiz with answers open.
Quiz closed-book; restate running-timer = null end.
Interleaved quiz; write the overlap query from blank.
Teach the approval state machine to someone.
Closed-book interleaved pass; misses restart Day 1.

blocks.callout: Retrieving just before you'd forget produces the strongest gain.
blocks.title: Why the gaps grow

blocks.p: Topics shuffled to train choosing the right idea.

items.q: M1. A user's totals are inflated; they have two running timers. How did it happen and how prevent it?
items.a: Concurrent start requests both created entries with null end. Prevent with a transactional auto-stop on start plus a partial unique index on user_id WHERE ended_at IS NULL.

items.q: M2. Finance changed the project rate and last month's invoice totals changed too. Bug?
items.a: Rate was read live instead of snapshotted. Store the resolved rate on each entry at creation so historical amounts are immutable.

items.q: M3. Reports are slow over a year of entries. Two fixes?
items.a: (1) Aggregate in SQL (SUM/GROUP BY) with indexes on (user_id/project_id, started_at). (2) Cache or precompute period rollups; don't loop entries in PHP.

items.q: M4. Billable amounts are a few cents off expectations. Likely cause?
items.a: Rounding applied inconsistently or to stored rounded durations. Keep true durations and apply one rounding policy at calculation time (e.g. ceil to nearest 6 min).

items.q: M5. An approved timesheet got edited and re-billed. What was missing?
items.a: A lock on approved entries. The approval state machine must forbid edits once approved (reopen only via reject → draft).

items.q: M6. When is an event-log model (start/stop events) better than start/end columns?
items.a: When you need a full audit trail of pauses/resumes and tamper evidence. It's more complex but reconstructs any state; for most apps start/end columns with derived duration are simpler and sufficient.

ul: Build duration two ways — derived from start/end vs a stored counter — and reproduce the drift/double-count bug.
Break the single-timer rule on purpose — remove the unique index, hammer the start endpoint concurrently, watch two running timers appear.
Write rounding + overlap tests first across edge cases (zero-length, midnight crossing, back-to-back).
Add idle detection — prompt to discard idle minutes on long-running timers.
Stretch goals: invoicing from approved timesheets, per-client contracts and rate cards, time-off/leave integration, and a 'pomodoro' running-timer UI fed by a single source-of-truth query.

flashcards: The hard parts of a time tracker
Running-timer state, overlap/rounding correctness, and fast aggregation.
How a running timer is stored
A time entry with ended_at = null — the single source of truth; duration is derived.
Why not an is_running flag
It can desync from reality; the null end can't contradict itself.
Enforcing one active timer
Transactional auto-stop on start + partial unique index on user_id WHERE ended_at IS NULL.
Interval overlap condition
newStart < existingEnd AND newEnd > existingStart (scoped to the user).
Why round at calc time
Rounding is a changeable billing policy; keep true duration, apply the rule when computing amounts.
Why snapshot the rate
So later rate changes don't rewrite historical billable amounts.
Rate precedence example
task.rate ?? project.rate ?? user.default_rate, resolved once and stored.
Timesheet states
draft → submitted → approved/rejected; approved entries locked against edits.
Reporting rule
Aggregate in SQL (SUM/GROUP BY, COALESCE now() for running) with indexes; cache rollups.
Computing live 'today' total
Cheap query over today's entries plus the running timer's now()-delta in the app.
When to use an event log
Audit-heavy needs (pauses/resumes, tamper evidence); otherwise start/end columns suffice.
glossary: Time entry
An interval of work with start, optional end, rate, and billable flag.
Running timer
A time entry whose ended_at is null.
Duration
Derived elapsed time = ended_at (or now) − started_at.
Overlap
Two of a user's entries covering the same instant.
Rounding rule
Policy mapping raw duration to billable units (e.g. nearest 6 min).
Rate snapshot
The rate stored on an entry at creation for immutable billing.
Billable
Whether an entry contributes to invoiceable amounts.
Timesheet
A period's entries moving through approval.
Approval workflow
State machine gating entries before invoicing.
Aggregation
SQL roll-up of hours by project/user/day.
Idle detection
Spotting inactive time to discard from a running timer.
Partial unique index
A DB constraint applying only to rows matching a condition.
